subject: Chemistry
question_text: Glucose or sucrose are soluble in water but cyclohexane or benzene (simple six membered ring compounds) are insoluble in water. Explain.
question_html: Glucose or sucrose are soluble in water but cyclohexane or benzene (simple six membered ring compounds) are insoluble in water. Explain.
solution_text: A glucose molecule contains five -OH groups while a sucrose molecule contains eight -OH groups. Thus, glucose and sucrose undergo extensive H-bonding with water. Hence, these are soluble in water. But cyclohexane and benzene do not contain -OH groups. Hence, they cannot undergo H-bonding with water and as a result, are insoluble in water.
solution_html:
A glucose molecule contains five -OH groups while a sucrose molecule contains eight -OH groups. Thus, glucose and sucrose undergo extensive H-bonding with water. Hence, these are soluble in water.
But cyclohexane and benzene do not contain -OH groups. Hence, they cannot undergo H-bonding with water and as a result, are insoluble in water.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What are the expected products of hydrolysis of lactose?
question_html: What are the expected products of hydrolysis of lactose?
solution_text: Lactose is composed of {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-galactose and {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-glucose. Thus, on hydrolysis, it gives {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-galactose and {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-glucose. {tex}\mathop {{{\text{C}}_{{\text{12}}}}{{\text{H}}_{{\text{22}}}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{11}}}}}\limits_{{\text{Lactose}}} {\text{ + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}} \to \mathop {{{\text{C}}_{\text{6}}}{{\text{H}}_{{\text{12}}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{6}}}}\limits_{{\text{D - ( + ) - Glucose}}} {\text{ + }}\mathop {{{\text{C}}_{\text{6}}}{{\text{H}}_{{\text{12}}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{6}}}}\limits_{{\text{D - ( + )Glucose}}} {/tex}
solution_html:
Lactose is composed of {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-galactose and {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-glucose. Thus, on hydrolysis, it gives {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-galactose and {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-glucose.

{tex}\mathop {{{\text{C}}_{{\text{12}}}}{{\text{H}}_{{\text{22}}}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{11}}}}}\limits_{{\text{Lactose}}} {\text{ + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}} \to \mathop {{{\text{C}}_{\text{6}}}{{\text{H}}_{{\text{12}}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{6}}}}\limits_{{\text{D - ( + ) - Glucose}}} {\text{ + }}\mathop {{{\text{C}}_{\text{6}}}{{\text{H}}_{{\text{12}}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{6}}}}\limits_{{\text{D - ( + )Glucose}}} {/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: How do you explain the absence of aldehyde group in the pentaacetate of D-glucose?
question_html: How do you explain the absence of aldehyde group in the pentaacetate of D-glucose?
solution_text: D-glucose reacts with hydroxylamine NH 2 OH to form an oxime because of the presence of aldehydic (-CHO) group or carbonyl carbon. This happens as the cyclic structure of glucose forms an open chain structure in an aqueous medium, which then reacts with NH 2 OH to give an oxime. But pentaacetate of D-glucose does not react with NH 2 OH. This is because pentaacetate does not form an open chain structure.
solution_html:
D-glucose reacts with hydroxylamine NH2OH to form an oxime because of the presence of aldehydic (-CHO) group or carbonyl carbon. This happens as the cyclic structure of glucose forms an open chain structure in an aqueous medium, which then reacts with NH2OH to give an oxime.

But pentaacetate of D-glucose does not react with NH2OH. This is because pentaacetate does not form an open chain structure.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The melting points and solubility in water of amino acids are generally higher than that of the corresponding halo acids. Explain.
question_html: The melting points and solubility in water of amino acids are generally higher than that of the corresponding halo acids. Explain.
solution_text: Both acidic (carboxyl) as well as basic (amino) groups are present in the same molecule of amino acids. In aqueous solutions, the carboxyl group can lose a proton and the amino group can accept a proton, thus giving rise to a dipolar ion known as a zwitter ion. Due to this dipolar behaviour, they have strong electrostatic interactions within them and with water. But halo-acids do not exhibit such dipolar behaviour. For this reason, the melting points and the solubility of amino acids in water is higher than those of the corresponding halo-acids.
solution_html:
Both acidic (carboxyl) as well as basic (amino) groups are present in the same molecule of amino acids. In aqueous solutions, the carboxyl group can lose a proton and the amino group can accept a proton, thus giving rise to a dipolar ion known as a zwitter ion.

Due to this dipolar behaviour, they have strong electrostatic interactions within them and with water. But halo-acids do not exhibit such dipolar behaviour.
For this reason, the melting points and the solubility of amino acids in water is higher than those of the corresponding halo-acids.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Where does the water present in the egg go after boiling the egg?
question_html: Where does the water present in the egg go after boiling the egg?
solution_text: When the egg is boiled, the proteins first undergo denaturation and then coagulation and the water present in the egg get absorbed.
solution_html: When the egg is boiled, the proteins first undergo denaturation and then coagulation and the water present in the egg get absorbed.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Why cannot vitamin C be stored in our body?
question_html: Why cannot vitamin C be stored in our body?
solution_text: Vitamin C cannot be stored in our body because it is water soluble. As a result, it is readily excreted in the urine.
solution_html: Vitamin C cannot be stored in our body because it is water soluble. As a result, it is readily excreted in the urine.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What products would be formed when a nucleotide from DNA containing thymine is hydrolysed?
question_html: What products would be formed when a nucleotide from DNA containing thymine is hydrolysed?
solution_text: When a nucleotide from the DNA containing thymine is hydrolyzed, thymine {tex}{\rm{\beta }}{/tex} -D-2-deoxyribose and phosphoric acid are obtained as products.
solution_html: When a nucleotide from the DNA containing thymine is hydrolyzed, thymine {tex}{\rm{\beta }}{/tex} -D-2-deoxyribose and phosphoric acid are obtained as products.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: When RNA is hydrolysed, there is no relationship among the quantities of different bases obtained. What does this fact suggest about the structure of RNA?
question_html: When RNA is hydrolysed, there is no relationship among the quantities of different bases obtained. What does this fact suggest about the structure of RNA?
solution_text: A DNA molecule is double-stranded in which the pairing of bases occurs. Adenine always pairs with thymine, while cytosine always pairs with guanine. Therefore, on hydrolysis of DNA, the quantity of adenine produced is equal to that of thymine and similarly, the quantity of cytosine is equal to that of guanine. But when RNA is hydrolyzed, there is no relationship among the quantities of the different bases obtained. Hence, RNA is single-stranded.
solution_html:
A DNA molecule is double-stranded in which the pairing of bases occurs. Adenine always pairs with thymine, while cytosine always pairs with guanine. Therefore, on hydrolysis of DNA, the quantity of adenine produced is equal to that of thymine and similarly, the quantity of cytosine is equal to that of guanine.
But when RNA is hydrolyzed, there is no relationship among the quantities of the different bases obtained. Hence, RNA is single-stranded.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What are monosaccharides?
question_html: What are monosaccharides?
solution_text: Monosaccharides are simple carbohydrates that cannot be hydrolysed, e.g. glucose, fructose, galactose, ribose, deoxyribose etc.
solution_html: Monosaccharides are simple carbohydrates that cannot be hydrolysed, e.g. glucose, fructose, galactose, ribose, deoxyribose etc.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What are reducing sugars?
question_html: What are reducing sugars?
solution_text: All those carbohydrates which reduce Fehling's solution and Tollen's reagent are referred to as reducing sugars. All monosaccharides whether aldose or ketose are reducing sugars.
solution_html: All those carbohydrates which reduce Fehling's solution and Tollen's reagent are referred to as reducing sugars. All monosaccharides whether aldose or ketose are reducing sugars.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Write two main functions of carbohydrates in plants.
question_html: Write two main functions of carbohydrates in plants.
solution_text: Two main functions of carbohydrates in plants are: Polysaccharides such as starch serve as storage molecules. Cellulose, a polysaccharide, is used to build the cell wall.
solution_html:
Two main functions of carbohydrates in plants are:

  1. Polysaccharides such as starch serve as storage molecules.
  2. Cellulose, a polysaccharide, is used to build the cell wall.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Classify the following into monosaccharides and disaccharides : Ribose, 2-deoxyribose, maltose, galactose, fructose and lactose
question_html:
Classify the following into monosaccharides and disaccharides :
Ribose, 2-deoxyribose, maltose, galactose, fructose and lactose
solution_text: Monosaccharides: Ribose, 2-deoxyribose, galactose, fructose Disaccharides: Maltose, lactose
solution_html:
Monosaccharides: Ribose, 2-deoxyribose, galactose, fructose
Disaccharides: Maltose, lactose

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What do you understand by the term glycosidic linkage?
question_html: What do you understand by the term glycosidic linkage?
solution_text: Glycosidic linkage refers to the linkage formed between two monosaccharide units through an oxygen atom by the loss of a water molecule. For example, in a sucrose molecule, two monosaccharide units, {tex}\propto{/tex} -glucose and {tex}{\rm{\beta }}{/tex} -fructose, are joined together by a glycosidic linkage.
solution_html:
Glycosidic linkage refers to the linkage formed between two monosaccharide units through an oxygen atom by the loss of a water molecule.
For example, in a sucrose molecule, two monosaccharide units, {tex}\propto{/tex} -glucose and {tex}{\rm{\beta }}{/tex} -fructose, are joined together by a glycosidic linkage.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What is glycogen? How is it different from starch?
question_html: What is glycogen? How is it different from starch?
solution_text: Carbohydrates are stored in the animal body as glycogen. Glycogen is a condensation polymer of {tex}\alpha{/tex} - D glucose. Starch is not a single compound but is a mixture of two components-a water-soluble components called amylose (15 - 20%) and water-insoluble component amylopectin (80 - 85%). Amylose is a linear polymer of {tex}\alpha{/tex} - D - glucose. But both glycogen and amylopectin are branched polymers of {tex}\alpha{/tex} - D - glucose; glycogen is more highly branched than amylopectin as amylopectin chains consist of 20 - 25 glucose units, glycogen chains consist of 10 - 14 glucose units.
solution_html:
Carbohydrates are stored in the animal body as glycogen. Glycogen is a condensation polymer of {tex}\alpha{/tex} - D glucose.
Starch is not a single compound but is a mixture of two components-a water-soluble components called amylose (15 - 20%) and water-insoluble component amylopectin (80 - 85%).
Amylose is a linear polymer of {tex}\alpha{/tex} - D - glucose. But both glycogen and amylopectin are branched polymers of {tex}\alpha{/tex} - D - glucose; glycogen is more highly branched than amylopectin as amylopectin chains consist of 20 - 25 glucose units, glycogen chains consist of 10 - 14 glucose units.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What are the hydrolysis products of sucrose and lactose
question_html:
What are the hydrolysis products of

  1. sucrose and
  2. lactose
solution_text: On hydrolysis, sucrose gives one molecule of {tex}\propto{/tex} -D glucose and one molecule of {tex}{\beta }{/tex} -fructose. The hydrolysis of lactose gives {tex}{\beta }{/tex} -galactose and {tex}{\beta }{/tex} -glucose.
solution_html:
1. On hydrolysis, sucrose gives one molecule of {tex}\propto{/tex} -D glucose and one molecule of {tex}{\beta }{/tex} -fructose.
  2. The hydrolysis of lactose gives {tex}{\beta }{/tex} -galactose and {tex}{\beta }{/tex} -glucose.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What is the basic structural difference between starch and cellulose ?
question_html: What is the basic structural difference between starch and cellulose ?
solution_text: Starch: It is a branched chain polymer of α-glucose and consists of two components: Amylose(water soluble) and Amylopectin (water insoluble). Cellulose: It is a straight chain polysaccharide composed only of β-D-glucose units which are joined by glycosidic linkage between C 1 of one glucose unit and C 4 of the next glucose unit.
solution_html:
Starch: It is a branched chain polymer of α-glucose and consists of two components: Amylose(water soluble) and Amylopectin (water insoluble).
Cellulose: It is a straight chain polysaccharide composed only of β-D-glucose units which are joined by glycosidic linkage between C1 of one glucose unit and C4 of the next glucose unit.

subject: Chemistry

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Enumerate the reactions of D-glucose which cannot be explained by its open chain structure.
question_html: Enumerate the reactions of D-glucose which cannot be explained by its open chain structure.
solution_text: Aldehydes give 2, 4-DNP test, Schiff's test, and react with NaHSO 4 to form the hydrogen sulphite addition product. However, glucose does not undergo these reactions. The pentaacetate of glucose does not react with hydroxylamine. This indicates that a free -CHO group is absent from glucose. Glucose exists in two crystalline forms - {tex}\propto{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} . The form (m.p. = 419 K) crystallises from a concentrated solution of glucose at 303 K and the {tex}{\rm{\beta }}{/tex} form (m.p = 423 K) crystallises from a hot and saturated aqueous solution at 371 K. This behavior cannot be explained by the open chain structure of glucose.
solution_html:
1. Aldehydes give 2, 4-DNP test, Schiff's test, and react with NaHSO4 to form the hydrogen sulphite addition product. However, glucose does not undergo these reactions.
  2. The pentaacetate of glucose does not react with hydroxylamine. This indicates that a free -CHO group is absent from glucose.
  3. Glucose exists in two crystalline forms -{tex}\propto{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex}. The form (m.p. = 419 K) crystallises from a concentrated solution of glucose at 303 K and the {tex}{\rm{\beta }}{/tex} form (m.p = 423 K) crystallises from a hot and saturated aqueous solution at 371 K. This behavior cannot be explained by the open chain structure of glucose.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What are essential and non-essential amino acids? Give two examples of each type.
question_html: What are essential and non-essential amino acids? Give two examples of each type.
solution_text: Essential amino acids are required by the human body, but they cannot be synthesised in the body. They must be taken through food. For example: valine and leucine. Non-essential amino acids are also required by the human body, but they can be synthesised in the body. For example: glycine, and alanine
solution_html:
Essential amino acids are required by the human body, but they cannot be synthesised in the body. They must be taken through food. For example: valine and leucine.
Non-essential amino acids are also required by the human body, but they can be synthesised in the body. For example: glycine, and alanine

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Define the following as related to proteins. Peptide linkage Primary structure Denaturation
question_html:
Define the following as related to proteins.

  1. Peptide linkage
  2. Primary structure
  3. Denaturation
solution_text: Peptide linkage. Amino acids are bifunctional molecules with NH 2 group at one end and COOH at the other. Therefore, the COOH of one molecule and NH 2 of another molecule interact by elimination of H 2 O to form an amide like linkage called peptide bond or peptide linkage. Primary structure. The sequence in which amino acids are linked with each other in polypeptide chain form primary structure. Denaturation. The process of by which secondary and tertiary structure of proteins get disturbed on change of pH or temperature and they are not able to perform their functions. This is called denaturation of proteins.
solution_html:
1. Peptide linkage. Amino acids are bifunctional molecules with NH2 group at one end and COOH at the other. Therefore, the COOH of one molecule and NH2 of another molecule interact by elimination of H2O to form an amide like linkage called peptide bond or peptide linkage.
  2. Primary structure. The sequence in which amino acids are linked with each other in polypeptide chain form primary structure.
  3. Denaturation. The process of by which secondary and tertiary structure of proteins get disturbed on change of pH or temperature and they are not able to perform their functions. This is called denaturation of proteins.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What are the common types of secondary structure of proteins?
question_html: What are the common types of secondary structure of proteins?
solution_text: There are two common types of secondary structure of proteins: {tex}\propto{/tex} -helix structure {tex}{\rm{\beta }}{/tex} pleated sheet structure {tex}\propto{/tex} - Helix structure In this structure, the {tex}-NH{/tex} -NH group of an amino acid residue forms H-bond with the group of the adjacent turn of the right-handed screw ( {tex}\propto{/tex} -helix). {tex}{\rm{\beta }}{/tex} pleated sheet structure: This structure is called so because it looks like the pleated folds of drapery. In this structure, all the peptide chains are stretched out to nearly the maximum extension and then laid side by side. These peptide chains are held together by intermolecular hydrogen bonds.
solution_html:
There are two common types of secondary structure of proteins:

  1. {tex}\propto{/tex} -helix structure
  2. {tex}{\rm{\beta }}{/tex} pleated sheet structure

{tex}\propto{/tex} - Helix structure
In this structure, the {tex}-NH{/tex} -NH group of an amino acid residue forms H-bond with the group of the adjacent turn of the right-handed screw ({tex}\propto{/tex} -helix).

{tex}{\rm{\beta }}{/tex} pleated sheet structure:
This structure is called so because it looks like the pleated folds of drapery. In this structure, all the peptide chains are stretched out to nearly the maximum extension and then laid side by side. These peptide chains are held together by intermolecular hydrogen bonds.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What types of bonding helps in stabilizing the {tex}\alpha {/tex} -helix structure of proteins?
question_html: What types of bonding helps in stabilizing the {tex}\alpha {/tex}-helix structure of proteins?
solution_text: Hydrogen bonding stabilize {tex}\alpha {/tex} -helix structure of proteins.
solution_html: Hydrogen bonding stabilize {tex}\alpha {/tex}-helix structure of proteins.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Differentiate between globular and fibrous proteins.
question_html: Differentiate between globular and fibrous proteins.
solution_text: Fibrous protein Globular protein 1. It is a fibre-like structure formed by the polypeptide chain. These proteins are held together by strong hydrogen and disulphide bonds. 1. The polypeptide chain in this protein is folded around itself, giving rise to a spherical structure. 2. It is usually insoluble in water. 2. It is usually soluble in water. 3. Fibrous proteins are usually used for structural purposes. For example, keratin is present in nails and hair; collagen in tendons; and myosin in muscles. 3. All enzymes are globular proteins. Some hormones such as insulin are also globular proteins.
solution_html:
Fibrous protein

Globular protein

1. It is a fibre-like structure formed by the polypeptide chain. These proteins are held together by strong hydrogen and disulphide bonds.

1. The polypeptide chain in this protein is folded around itself, giving rise to a spherical structure.

2. It is usually insoluble in water.

2. It is usually soluble in water.

3. Fibrous proteins are usually used for structural purposes. For example, keratin is present in nails and hair; collagen in tendons; and myosin in muscles.

3. All enzymes are globular proteins. Some hormones such as insulin are also globular proteins.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: How do you explain the amphoteric behaviour of amino acids?
question_html: How do you explain the amphoteric behaviour of amino acids?
solution_text: Amino acids contain anacidic (carbonyl group) and a basic (amino group) within the same molecule. In zwitter ionic form {tex}\alpha{/tex} -amino acids show amphoteric behaviour as they react with both acids and bases.
solution_html: Amino acids contain anacidic (carbonyl group) and a basic (amino group) within the same molecule. In zwitter ionic form {tex}\alpha{/tex}-amino acids show amphoteric behaviour as they react with both acids and bases.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What are enzymes?
question_html: What are enzymes?
solution_text: Enzymes are proteins that catalyse biological reactions. They are very specific in nature and catalyse only a particular reaction for a particular substrate. Enzymes are usually named after the particular substrate or class of substrate and sometimes after the particular reaction. For example, the enzyme used to catalyse the hydrolysis of maltose into glucose is named as maltase. {tex}\mathop {{C_{12}}{H_{22}}{O_{11}}}\limits_{Maltose} \xrightarrow[]{Maltase}\mathop {2{C_6}{H_{12}}{O_6}}\limits_{Glu\cos e} {/tex} Again, the enzymes used to catalyse the oxidation of one substrate with the simultaneous reduction of another substrate are named as oxidoreductase enzymes. The name of an enzyme ends with {tex}- {\rm{ase}}'{/tex} .
solution_html:
Enzymes are proteins that catalyse biological reactions. They are very specific in nature and catalyse only a particular reaction for a particular substrate. Enzymes are usually named after the particular substrate or class of substrate and sometimes after the particular reaction.
For example, the enzyme used to catalyse the hydrolysis of maltose into glucose is named as maltase.
{tex}\mathop {{C_{12}}{H_{22}}{O_{11}}}\limits_{Maltose} \xrightarrow[]{Maltase}\mathop {2{C_6}{H_{12}}{O_6}}\limits_{Glu\cos e} {/tex}
Again, the enzymes used to catalyse the oxidation of one substrate with the simultaneous reduction of another substrate are named as oxidoreductase enzymes.
The name of an enzyme ends with {tex}- {\rm{ase}}'{/tex}.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What is the effect of denaturation on the structure of proteins?
question_html: What is the effect of denaturation on the structure of proteins?
solution_text: During the denaturation 2° and 3° structure of proteins are destroyed but 1° structure remain intact. For example curding of milk.
solution_html: During the denaturation 2° and 3° structure of proteins are destroyed but 1° structure remain intact. For example curding of milk.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: How are vitamins classified? Name the vitamin responsible for the coagulation of blood.
question_html: How are vitamins classified? Name the vitamin responsible for the coagulation of blood.
solution_text: On the basis of their solubility in water or fat, vitamins are classified into two groups. Fat-soluble vitamins: Vitamins that are soluble in fat and oils, but not in water, belong to this group. For example: Vitamins A, D, E, and K. Water-soluble vitamins: Vitamins that are soluble in water belong to this group. For example: B group vitamins ( {tex}{{\rm{B}}_1},{{\rm{B}}_2},{{\rm{B}}_6},{{\rm{B}}_{12}}{/tex} , etc.) and vitamin C However, biotin or vitamin H is neither soluble in water nor in fat. Vitamin K is responsible for the coagulation of blood.
solution_html:
On the basis of their solubility in water or fat, vitamins are classified into two groups.

  1. Fat-soluble vitamins: Vitamins that are soluble in fat and oils, but not in water, belong to this group. For example: Vitamins A, D, E, and K.
  2. Water-soluble vitamins: Vitamins that are soluble in water belong to this group. For example: B group vitamins ({tex}{{\rm{B}}_1},{{\rm{B}}_2},{{\rm{B}}_6},{{\rm{B}}_{12}}{/tex} , etc.) and vitamin C

However, biotin or vitamin H is neither soluble in water nor in fat.
Vitamin K is responsible for the coagulation of blood.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Why are vitamin A and vitamin C essential to us? Give their important sources.
question_html: Why are vitamin A and vitamin C essential to us? Give their important sources.
solution_text: The deficiency of vitamin A leads to xerophthalmia (hardening of the cornea of the eye) and night blindness. The deficiency of vitamin C leads to scurvy (bleeding gums). The sources of vitamin A are fish liver oil, carrots, butter, and milk. The sources of vitamin C are citrus fruits, amla, and green leafy vegetables.
solution_html:
The deficiency of vitamin A leads to xerophthalmia (hardening of the cornea of the eye) and night blindness. The deficiency of vitamin C leads to scurvy (bleeding gums).
The sources of vitamin A are fish liver oil, carrots, butter, and milk. The sources of vitamin C are citrus fruits, amla, and green leafy vegetables.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What are nucleic acids? Mention their two important functions.
question_html: What are nucleic acids? Mention their two important functions.
solution_text: Nucleic acids are polymer of nucleotides containing pentose sugar, heterocyclic base and a phosphate group. They help in synthesis of proteins. They are responsible for transfer of genetic characters.
solution_html:
Nucleic acids are polymer of nucleotides containing pentose sugar, heterocyclic base and a phosphate group.
They help in synthesis of proteins.
They are responsible for transfer of genetic characters.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What is the difference between a nucleoside and a nucleotide?
question_html: What is the difference between a nucleoside and a nucleotide?
solution_text: A nucleoside is formed by the attachment of a base to position of sugar. Nucleoside = Sugar + Base On the other hand, all the three basic components of nucleic acids (i.e., pentose sugar, phosphoric acid, and base) are present in a nucleotide. Nucleotide = Sugar + Base + Phosphoric acid
solution_html:
A nucleoside is formed by the attachment of a base to position of sugar.
Nucleoside = Sugar + Base

On the other hand, all the three basic components of nucleic acids (i.e., pentose sugar, phosphoric acid, and base) are present in a nucleotide.
Nucleotide = Sugar + Base + Phosphoric acid

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The two strands in DNA are not identical but are complementary. Explain.
question_html: The two strands in DNA are not identical but are complementary. Explain.
solution_text: In the helical structure of DNA, the two strands are held together by hydrogen bonds between specific pairs of bases. Cytosine forms hydrogen bond with guanine, while adenine forms hydrogen bond with thymine. As a result, the two strands are complementary to each other.
solution_html: In the helical structure of DNA, the two strands are held together by hydrogen bonds between specific pairs of bases. Cytosine forms hydrogen bond with guanine, while adenine forms hydrogen bond with thymine. As a result, the two strands are complementary to each other.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Write the important structural and functional differences between DNA and RNA.
question_html: Write the important structural and functional differences between DNA and RNA.
solution_text: The structural differences between DNA and RNA are as follows: DNA RNA 1. The sugar moiety in DNA molecules is {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-2 deoxyribose. 1. The sugar moiety in RNA molecules is {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-ribose. 2. DNA contains thymine (T). It does not contain uracil (U). 2. RNA contains uracil (U). It does not contain thymine (T). 3. The helical structure of DNA is double-stranded. 3. The helical structure of RNA is single-stranded. The functional differences between DNA and RNA are as follows: DNA RNA 1. DNA is the chemical basis of heredity. 1. RNA is not responsible for heredity. 2. DNA molecules do not synthesise proteins, but transfer coded message for the synthesis of proteins in the cells. 2. Proteins are synthesised by RNA molecules in the cells.
solution_html:
The structural differences between DNA and RNA are as follows:

DNA

RNA

1. The sugar moiety in DNA molecules is {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-2 deoxyribose.

1. The sugar moiety in RNA molecules is {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-ribose.

2. DNA contains thymine (T). It does not contain uracil (U).

2. RNA contains uracil (U). It does not contain thymine (T).

3. The helical structure of DNA is double-stranded.

3. The helical structure of RNA is single-stranded.


The functional differences between DNA and RNA are as follows:

DNA

RNA

1. DNA is the chemical basis of heredity.

1. RNA is not responsible for heredity.

2. DNA molecules do not synthesise proteins, but transfer coded message for the synthesis of proteins in the cells.

2. Proteins are synthesised by RNA molecules in the cells.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What are the different types of RNA found in the cell?
question_html: What are the different types of RNA found in the cell?
solution_text: There are three types of RNA: Ribosomal RNA (r-RNA) Messenger RNA (m-RNA) Transfer RNA (t-RNA)
solution_html:
There are three types of RNA:

  1. Ribosomal RNA (r-RNA)
  2. Messenger RNA (m-RNA)
  3. Transfer RNA (t-RNA)
